full_response:
Step 1   Write both line elements in the general form  
For a two–dimensional surface whose coordinates are (u,v) we can always write  
  ds² = du² + f²(u) dv².

• Euclidean plane in polar coordinates (r,φ): f(r)=r  
  ds² = dr² + r² dφ².

• Lobachevsky (hyperbolic) plane in geodesic‐polar coordinates (u,v): f(u)=cosh u  
  ds² = du² + cosh²u dv².

Step 2   Compute the Gaussian curvature  
For a metric of the form ds² = du² + f²(u) dv² the Gaussian curvature is

  K = − f ''(u) / f(u).

– Euclidean: f(r)=r  ⇒ f ''(r)=0 ⇒ K = 0.  
– Lobachevsky: f(u)=cosh u ⇒ f ''(u)=cosh u ⇒ K = −1 (constant negative curvature).

Thus the two surfaces have intrinsically different curvatures.

Step 3   Show they cannot be related by a coordinate change in the plane  
Assume one could find r=r(u) such that

  dr² + r² dφ² = du² + cosh²u dv²

with φ = v.  Comparing the du² terms gives (dr/du)² = 1 ⇒ r = u + const.  
Inserting this into the dv² terms would require u² = cosh²u, which is impossible except at u=0.  
Therefore no re-labeling of coordinates within the same surface can convert one metric into the other.

Step 4   Physical/Geometrical interpretation  
In flat space the circumference of a circle of radius r is 2πr.  
In the Lobachevsky plane the circumference of a circle of geodesic radius u is 2π cosh u ≈ 2π(e^u/2) for large u, i.e. it grows exponentially with the radius.  
This faster–than–linear growth and the corresponding metric coefficient cosh²u are direct consequences of the negative curvature of the hyperbolic plane.

Hence the “difference’’ between the two metrics is not a matter of coordinates; it reflects the fact that the Lobachevsky plane is a space of constant negative curvature whereas the ordinary Euclidean plane is flat (zero curvature).

\boxed{\text{The Lobachevsky metric ds}^{2}=du^{2}+\cosh ^{2}u\,dv^{2}\text{ describes a surface of constant negative curvature }(K=-1),\text{ while the Euclidean metric ds}^{2}=dr^{2}+r^{2}d\phi ^{2}\text{ is flat }(K=0);\;the different functional factors (\cosh u vs.\;r) encode this intrinsic curvature, so the two line elements cannot be transformed into each other by any coordinate change— the spaces themselves are geometrically different.}

Confidence: 80%
extracted_answer: \text{The Lobachevsky metric ds}^{2}=du^{2}+\cosh ^{2}u\,dv^{2}\text{ describes a surface of constant negative curvature }(K=-1),\text{ while the Euclidean metric ds}^{2}=dr^{2}+r^{2}d\phi ^{2}\text{ is flat }(K=0);\;the different functional factors (\cosh u vs.\;r) encode this intrinsic curvature, so the two line elements cannot be transformed into each other by any coordinate change— the spaces themselves are geometrically different.
